speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 30th, 1995. We're interviewing Esther Hautzig Rudomin. My name is Amy Margolis-Cohen. No, it's backwards. It's Rudomin Hautzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine. That's OK. Do you want to start over?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we do it again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: It should be right, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, what this is, is we're--

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 30th, 1995. We're interviewing Esther Rudomin Hautzig. Rudomin is the maiden name. The interviewer is Amy Margolis-Cohen. The interview is taking place in New York, in the United States of America, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Amy Margolis-Cohen-- M-A-R-G-O-L-I-S C-O-H-E-N. It's March 30th, 1995. We're interviewing Esther Rudomin Hautzig in New York City, and the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do we start?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why are you tapping me?

speaker: interviewer
text: To start.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're interviewing Mrs. Esther Rudomin Hautzig. Would you please spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Esther Rudomin Hautzig. Esther-- E-S-T-H-E-R. Rudomin-- R-U-D-O-M-I-N. Hautzig-- H-A-U-T-Z-I-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on October 18th, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vilna, Poland. The city is called Vilna by the Jews, mostly-- Vilno by the Poles, and Vilnius by the Lithuanians. But when I was born there, it was Poland, so to the Poles it was Vilno, and to the Jews it was Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you please paint for us a very clear picture of what your childhood was like? What was it like growing up in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: To grow up in Vilna, for me, was like to live in perpetual heaven, until the age of nine. I was born on a Saturday. And I always think of what Saturday's child is supposed to do. It's supposed to work very hard for a living. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was born to a family that was built in two parallel ways. My mother was a Yiddishist. She was interested in cultural affairs. She was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a 'doer.' My father was a 'doer' in different ways. He was a merchant. He was also trained as an electrical engineer. And my father's business was very large and very prosperous, on one of the main streets of Vilna. It was called, in Polish, [POLISH] [? Domynski-- ?] the [? 'Domynski' ?] to make it more Polish-sounding than Rudomin. My mother's maiden name was Zunser, which is a very well-known name among Yiddish-speaking Jews. Her great-great-uncle was Elyokem Zunser, who was a famous badkhn. And a badkhn was a poet who composed songs with the words on the spot at weddings, and bar mitzvahs, and special occasions. So my mother was Chaja-Rivka Zunser, and she was called [? Raja ?] Zunser Rudomin. And I was one of 11 cousins living in the same building. We had what you might call garden apartments around a big courtyard, with beautiful flowers, and a large lilac tree in front of my father's study. And when I was born, my parents planted a little white lilac bush next to this huge, beautiful, purple French lilac. And I never had to worry about having playmates, because they were all next door, and the door after that, and upstairs. And my grandparents lived across the stairwell. And it was an enclave. It was a self-contained little paradise. I went to school, to a Yiddish-speaking folkshul, which means that every subject was conducted in Yiddish. My mother graduated from a gymnasium, where all the subjects were also taught in Yiddish. And it was one of the few-- it was the only gymnasium in that part of the world, or in Poland, where the students who studied every subject in Yiddish could go straight to a state university, without having to go for a two-year program in a Polish-speaking school. We were just wealthy. I mean, I used to feel guilty about it, because in the folkshul which I attended, the tuition was paid by some of the wealthy children's families. But most of the students-- many of the students-- my school's name was Sofia Markovna Gurewicz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And many of the students came from sometimes impoverished homes, and sometimes middle class, lower middle class, working people. And I had a lot of friends who would come to my apartment, and I would have every toy in the world. And it was enormous by other standards. One of my best friends lived with her mother in one room. And I used to think, why do I have so much and they don't? And I don't say that was a good feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: But otherwise, aside from that, I just can't think of a more wonderful childhood. The city itself is so gorgeous now, and it was even more gorgeous then. You could just walk out the door, and walk a few streets, and you were in a park. And you walked five minutes more, and you were on top of a hill called Castle Hill-- which ruin-- which had the ruins of an old castle, which was actually the beginning of Vilnius. It was built by a Lithuanian-- it was founded by a Lithuanian prince in 1332 as a hunting lodge, and it's still there. And my cousins and my friends and I would just go up on top of this wonderful, you know, ready-made fairyland [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] set, if you will. It's like Hollywood. You walk there now, and it's like Hollywood. It's gorgeous. And so I was very happy. I had all I wanted to eat-- too much. I had all the toys I wanted. I had my wonderful cousins, and wonderful friends-- and grandparents, and aunts, and uncles, and everyone. And it was idyllic. I think when I die and go to heaven, heaven will be Vilna, as I knew it. I hope that gave you a word picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kinds of things, other than going to this castle, did you and your cousins do together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We played games. We went to the library. We invented games. We put on little shows. We had to garden. My grandfather assigned a piece of a flower bed. We had enormous flower beds in this courtyard of the house in which I grew up. And each cousin had a piece of land-- you know, maybe a foot by a foot-- and we had to plant a couple of carrots, and a couple of onions, and a couple of potatoes, and just make them as good as we could so that if things got bad, we would know how to raise food. And that took up a lot of time in the spring.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went to a dacha in the summer. In the fall, we had to harvest. And our grandfather would give us a prize every Erev Shabbas to see who did the best tending. I must say, gardening has never been [LAUGHS] not fraught with worry. Would my grandfather approve of my flowers now? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a time or a childhood where things were programmed. They just happened. I had a nanny-- a governess. And her name was, when she came to us, Rochele Taboryski. Rachel Taboryski-- T-A-B-O-R-Y-S-K-I. And Rochele in transliterated English would be R-O-K-H-E-L-L-E. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And she did things with me. You know, I never had to think, what am I going to do next? We just did things. We went for walks. We studied. We-- she read me stories. I had to tell stories. I think it all sort of-- I don't remember a set day.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I do remember one thing. Rochele had a chart by my bed for each day. And she would put on a star-- gold, silver, red, green, and-- I don't remember what the last one was. Gold was the best. Silver was not so terrific. Red was hm, mm, mm. Green was not good. So I was always aiming to have all gold stars for the five days of the week that she came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I succeeded a lot, and not all the time, though. It was just a good childhood without worries. It was happy. I had so many loving people around me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a religious group?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and father had a kosher home. But I don't think it was necessarily because of any deep religiosity, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the part of my mother, who was a Yiddishist, who was very Jewish. And my father, who was also extremely Jewish in his outlook and in his spirit, but not necessarily in shul-going. It was a Yiddish home, not a religious Jewish home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But culturally Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Culturally Jewish. My mother spoke Yiddish to most of her friends. But when they were-- so, you know, my father's friends and my mother's friends-- I heard a lot of Polish, because not all of them were Yiddishists. And many of them went to Polish-speaking gymnasiums. And some of my mother's-- some of my father's brothers' wives were Russian-speaking. So it was like polyglot language atmosphere. But I actually spoke only Polish, and then Yiddish. When I went to the Yiddish school, I didn't know a word of Yiddish. I had to start as if it was a foreign language.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six and 1/2. But in Sofia Markovna Gurewicz, we had everything in Yiddish, and Polish was the foreign language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate the Sabbath?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, of course. We lit candles and-- and had a Sabbath meal. And-- and on Saturdays, I sometimes went to shul with my grandfather. And he was like the Pied Piper with all the grandchildren-- the girls and the boys together. The girls went downstairs like the boys, when Grandfather took us. My mother didn't go to shul every Saturday, and my father did sometimes. But when I went to shul, mostly it was with my grandfather, and not with my parents. But for the holidays, we all went, and I sat with my mother upstairs, or behind the mechitza.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which grandfather is this, your paternal, or your maternal?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather-- the grandfather I refer to is my father's father, and his name was Shlomo Ben Shimon Rudomin. Actually, his name was Shlomo Mordechai Ben Shimon Rudomin. And the reason I am stressing this is that my father had this wonderful, wonderful habit of writing things down. And when he died, I found, among his papers, a list of every Yahrtzeit, of everyone that he knew who died natural deaths before the war. And that's how I knew that my grandfather was not just Shlomo-- Solomon-- but Shlomo Mordechai Ben Shimon. And when I went to look up genealogical records in Salt Lake City, in the Mormon genealogical library-- which has very little material on Vilna and on Poland, but I found a listing for Shimon Rudomin in a list of men from Vilna who gave money for Eretz Israel in 1891. And I found it in 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: See? So it's good to know the names of your ancestors, and to write down things for your children and grandchildren. Because I say kaddish for 16 people every year, and I buy a book in their memory.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I go to shul on Weston Avenue in the 100th Street to Anche Chesed, my synagogue-- A-N-C-H-E, Chesed C-H-E-S-E-D-- very often I pick up a book which says given in memory of Shlomo Mordechai Ben Shimon Rudomin. So it's really nice. And if my father hadn't written it all down very carefully, I might not have known. I wouldn't have known.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from a question-- were we religious-- we wind up with this, but it's all connected. Your questions lead in different directions. Can you tell me about the relationship you had with your father and the relationship you had with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, dear. My father was fun-loving. Before the war, during the war, after the war, into his 90th year, everything was wonderful, wonderful. And everything was splendid when-- when I was a little girl. And my mother was a serious person, very serious. Not judgmental, never punished me unless-- she never punished me. She never threatened me with punishment. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she wasn't kissy-huggy. I got all of that from Margola, and-- Margola being my mother's younger sister, M-A-R-G-O-L-A Zunser. And my father, and my grandmother, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: But boy was she strong, and was she the center of our household in Siberia. And before the war, she was equal. I mean, when came out in the feminist movement in-- in America, I said, what are they talking about? We lived like that in Vilna. My mother didn't work because, luckily, she didn't have to. But she would have if she had to. No decision was made because the father was the boss of the family. So I, you know, what is this? Even my daughter said to me-- she was 12-- 'I thought, Ma, I thought women get the same education, the same rights. Grandma did. And you say Great-Grandma did.' And I said, 'And your great, great grandma did. She was the head of a business.' So my mother had a lot of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] characteristics you think of as masculine, and my father had a lot of characteristics you might think as feminine. My father cried. My mother never cried. My father would cry if he was happy and if he was sad. My mother was always controlled. Nicht schlecht-- not bad-- was the biggest compliment. What was that like for you as a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it like for me as a little girl? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hmm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't a worry, because I didn't feel anything was lacking. I had my father, and I had all these uncles, and aunts, and cousins, and Miss Rachel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was this stillness, you know? She was this quiet, still, steady, intelligent, soft-spoken-- a woman not of many words, but when she said something, oh boy, you listened. Not only I listened-- everybody listened. Just before she died, someone said to me, do you know that your mother is not just the smartest person I know? She's the wisest person I know. And she said, do you know there is a difference? And I said, yes, I think I do. I didn't know when I was young, but I do now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father bought me a little toy doll bathroom. Why? Because his business did work in [NON-ENGLISH]. That means in laying water pipes. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what is it called when bathrooms and kitchens are built? You have to have water. Plumbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not plumbing-- the water-- the entry of the water into the-- oh, I don't know how to say it-- canalization. Whatever. He was involved with water getting to the places it was supposed to get to. I guess they had plumbing supplies, too. You're right. There were plumbing supplies in that store.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he bought me this bathroom. And if you-- there was a little thing in the back, and water went into it. And if you turned the spigot, the water came out into this little sink. I thought that was just heaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother saw this, and she said, that's useless. So we went and exchanged this heavenly toy for a very complicated geography game, and I still don't know where anything is. And maybe that's not important in an interview like this, but this was a real crisis. I mean, when I think of a crisis in my childhood, it was not having [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lack of bread or, God forbid, being ill, or some real crisis. But this was-- and I still think of it, and I'm 64. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Next one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: About seven. Six or seven. Yes. You don't forget things. Even little things can stay for a lifetime, and more important things kind of ebb away.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in school, and when you were running around Vilna with your cousins, did you experience any sort of antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I didn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But people did. I mean, I was a little girl, and I lived in a-- in a very protected environment. I never, never suffered from anti-Semitism. But the students at the university-- in university in Vilnius did-- Vilna, then. I know that they went on strike at one point. My mother's brother, Elle Chim, E-L-L-A, Chim C-H-I-M, Zunser, and Margola-- I spelled her name before-- they were at the university. And subsequently, I heard that they did suffer from antisemitic professors and hierarchy. But there were professors who protected the-- the Jewish students. My uncle's thesis advisor, Dr. Antoni Zygmund, A-N-T-O-N-I, Zygmund, Z-Y-G-M-U-N-D. Died only about two years ago. He taught at the University of Chicago, and was emeritus professor. And he was a very famous mathematician of the 20th century. And when he was a professor at the university in Vilna, where my uncle Liusik-- that's his nickname, L-I-U-S-I-K-- studied, he protected the Jewish students. A wonderful, wonderful man, and I'm glad I have the opportunity to give them the covet, the honor, of acknowledging it to your question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Margola and Liusik tell you about these problems when they were in school, or after?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never. No, I never heard about it until later. And not so long ago, from colleagues of Margola's that-- who survived, that there was antisemitism. My father said there were periods when he was in business that antisemitism was a real problem. They didn't want to do business with Jewish businesses. And my father's business was not just a shop. He represented SKF ball bearings from Eskilstuna, Sweden-- very large worldwide firm. He represented Goodyear Tires. He represented even Singer Sewing Machines. That meant that he was like the middleman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when governments changed-- the prime minister of Poland died, Joseph Pilsutski-- a new government came in and that was very difficult to deal with, my father told me. Don't forget, he lived to be 90, and I never tired of his stories whenever there was time. So what happened? So tell me again. And that's how-- maybe if I had heard it only once, I wouldn't remember. Yes. Who was your best friend when you were growing up? Did you have one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liusik, my cousin. He was my very best friend, and he was also my first cousin. And when I think of friends, I think of my cousins first. But I had friends in school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of my really good friend's nickname was Beba, and his last name was Szkolnicki, spelled S-Z-K-O-L-N-I-C-K-I. And we went one summer on vacation together-- his mother and my mother and I, to the seashore. And [INAUDIBLE] Szkolnicki, Beba's mother, survived the war. His father didn't, and he didn't. And Miss Rachel had the only pictures of Beba with me in that summer on the seashore. And I was able to send it to [INAUDIBLE] Szkolnicki in Paris. She married a man named Zajac, Z-A-J-A-C, after the war. And actually, he's the only friend I remember very clearly. The others are dimmer in my memory, because I had my cousins. Morning, noon, and night, and-- and [YIDDISH], you know, on Sabbaths and on all holidays, we were all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sounds like you had a really nice life. When did things start to change? And how did you notice something started to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- it wasn't just noticing. It was like a thunderbolt. In August '39, we were in the-- in the country-- dacha, it was called, you know, summer house. And you heard a lot about wojna, war-- wojna is war in Polish. And in the end of August, my father, at the age of over 39-- he was 40-- he was born in 1899-- was drafted into the Polish army. Drafted, a Jew, at 40 as a foot soldier. And he went off. And we saw him to the train, and he went to war. And then the Germans were coming from one direction, the Russians from the other, and Vilna was in the middle. And in 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Molotov, who was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think you would call him the Secretary of State in Stalin's government, and von Ribbentrop, who was in Hitler's government, arranged for a peace pact between Hitler and Stalin. And Vilna was given to the Russians. In other words, the Germans stopped just before. And we were in Vilna, but we didn't know where my father was. And finally news came that his dog tag-- you know, all soldiers wear an ID-- was found in a field, and it was assumed that he was killed. And my mother, who had this incredible, incredible sense of ESP, said no. He didn't die. He will come back. So everybody thought, poor woman, lost her senses. I remember when the rabbi from the synagogue came to call on her. She very politely said to him-- I don't remember exactly what she said. But I remember the scene as if it was last week. Don't-- don't worry. You don't have to offer your sympathy. I know Mula-- that was my father's nickname-- will come back. And I don't mean to sound [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dismissive of that, but she seemed to have dismissed the man. And he left. And my father did come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Change the tape.
